title: Types of testing
short_description: An overview map of how software testing is classified and how the major testing types relate to each other.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Classify a given test activity along the main axes: functional vs non-functional, manual vs automated, and black-box vs white-box.
Explain where each testing type sits in the software lifecycle (unit, integration, system, acceptance levels).
Pick the right testing type for a concrete situation (e.g. a hotfix, a new feature, a release candidate).
Draw the relationship between smoke, sanity, regression and retesting without confusing them.
theory:
Software testing is not one activity but a family of activities, and a QA engineer is expected to know which one to reach for and why. The most common high-level split is functional testing (does the system do what it should, by spec?) versus non-functional testing (how well does it do it: speed, security, usability, compatibility). A second axis is the test level: unit (a single function/class), integration (modules talking to each other), system (the whole assembled product) and acceptance (does it satisfy the business and the user).

A third axis is who or what executes the test. Manual testing means a human follows steps and judges the result; automated testing means a script (Selenium, Cypress, Playwright, pytest) checks it repeatedly. A fourth axis is knowledge of internals: black-box testing works only from the interface and requirements, white-box testing uses the source code and logic paths, and grey-box mixes both.

Within functional testing there is a recurring lifecycle of 'gate' tests. Smoke testing is a quick, broad check that a build is stable enough to test at all. Sanity testing is a narrow, deep check of one area after a small change. Regression testing re-runs existing tests to confirm new changes did not break old behavior. Retesting (confirmation testing) re-runs the specific failing test after a defect is supposedly fixed.

Why does the classification matter on the job? Because it drives planning. A release manager who asks for 'test coverage before go-live' expects smoke + regression. A developer who fixes bug JIRA-1234 expects retesting. A performance concern triggers load testing, not more functional cases. Knowing the map lets you scope work correctly and communicate using the same vocabulary as the rest of the team.

These categories overlap rather than being mutually exclusive. A single test such as 'log in with valid credentials and confirm the dashboard loads in under 2 seconds' is functional (login works), non-functional (2-second budget), black-box (no code needed) and could be run manually or automated. Treat the types as lenses, not boxes.
key_terms: functional testing, non-functional testing, black-box testing, white-box testing, test level, regression testing, smoke testing, acceptance testing
real_world_example: Your team is shipping a release on Friday. As QA you build a plan: a 15-minute smoke run on the new build (can users log in, load the home page, place an order?), then a full regression suite covering payments and checkout, then retesting of the three bugs fixed this sprint, and finally a quick performance check that the checkout page still responds within the agreed 3-second budget. Knowing the types lets you size and sequence this work instead of just 'clicking around'.
step_by_step:
1. Read the change request or release notes and list what actually changed.
2. Decide the test level affected: is it a single unit, an integration point, or the whole system?
3. Choose functional types: does new behavior need new test cases? Do fixed bugs need retesting? Does existing behavior need regression?
4. Decide if any non-functional concern applies (speed, security, compatibility, usability).
5. Decide manual vs automated for each: stable, repeatable checks lean automated; new or exploratory areas lean manual.
6. Order the work: smoke first (gate), then targeted tests, then regression, then non-functional.
7. Write a one-paragraph test plan naming each type you will run and why.
common_mistakes:
Confusing smoke and sanity testing, or using the words interchangeably.
Thinking regression and retesting are the same thing (regression = broad, old tests; retesting = the one specific fixed bug).
Assuming 'testing types' only means functional, and forgetting non-functional concerns until production breaks.
Treating the categories as rigid boxes instead of overlapping lenses.
Jumping straight to writing test cases without first identifying which type and level is even needed.
practical_use_case: Every sprint, when a QA receives a ticket or a build, they silently run this classification to scope their work: what level, functional or non-functional, what needs regression, what needs retesting. It also shapes how you write the test plan section of a release document and how you estimate QA effort.
summary: Testing is classified by purpose (functional/non-functional), level (unit/integration/system/acceptance), executor (manual/automated) and code knowledge (black/white/grey-box). The same test can belong to several categories at once. Knowing the map lets you scope, sequence and communicate QA work correctly.
example:
Test-type classification worksheet for ticket SHOP-482 'Add discount-code field to checkout':

| Activity | Functional/Non-func | Level | Manual/Auto | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Verify valid code applies discount | Functional | System | Manual then Auto | New feature test |
| Verify invalid code shows error | Functional | System | Manual | Negative test |
| Re-run checkout & payment suite | Functional | System | Auto | Regression |
| Re-verify old bug SHOP-461 still fixed | Functional | System | Manual | Retesting |
| Checkout loads under 3s with field added | Non-functional | System | Manual | Performance |
| Field readable on iPhone SE + Chrome | Non-functional | System | Manual | Compatibility |

Plan order: smoke build -> new feature tests -> negative tests -> regression -> retesting -> performance/compatibility.
interactive: You are handed a build that fixes one login bug and adds a 'Remember me' checkbox. List which testing types you would run, in order, and give one sentence of justification for each.
expected_answer: A strong answer names: 1) Smoke test the build first to confirm it is stable enough to test (login page loads, app boots). 2) New feature functional tests for the 'Remember me' checkbox (checked vs unchecked behavior, session persistence). 3) Retesting of the specific login bug that was fixed, using the original reproduction steps. 4) Regression testing around authentication and session areas to confirm nothing else broke. 5) Optionally a compatibility check if 'Remember me' uses cookies that behave differently across browsers. The ordering (smoke as a gate, retesting tied to the fixed bug, regression as a safety net) matters as much as the list.
homework: Take any app you use daily (email, banking, a shopping site). Write a one-page testing strategy that names at least six different testing types from this lesson, and for each explain in one or two sentences when you would apply it to that app and why.
expected_result: A one-page document that correctly distinguishes functional from non-functional types, includes at least one of each, and shows correct understanding of smoke vs sanity vs regression vs retesting. Each type should be tied to a concrete situation for the chosen app (e.g. 'regression after their monthly release', 'compatibility because users are on both iOS and Android'), not just defined abstractly.

quiz.question: Which pair correctly separates testing by PURPOSE rather than by who executes it?
A developer fixes bug JIRA-77 and asks you to confirm just that bug is gone. Which type is this primarily?
Which statement about the testing-type categories is most accurate?
answers: A single test can belong to several categories at once
Non-functional testing is a subset of unit testing
Black-box testing always requires the source code

quiz.question: Which testing type is the GATE you run first to decide whether a build is even worth deeper testing?

title: Functional testing
short_description: How to verify that each feature of the software behaves according to its requirements.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Derive functional test cases directly from a requirement or acceptance criterion.
Design positive and negative cases using boundary values and equivalence partitions.
Distinguish functional testing from non-functional testing with concrete examples.
Validate end-to-end flows (e.g. registration, checkout) against expected outputs.
theory:
Functional testing checks WHAT the system does: given an input and a precondition, does it produce the correct output and state, as defined by the requirements? It is black-box by nature, you do not need to read the code, only know what the feature is supposed to do. The reference is always the specification, user story, or acceptance criteria.

A functional test has a clear structure: preconditions, input/action, and expected result. For example, requirement 'a user must be at least 18 to register' yields cases at the boundary: age 17 (reject), 18 (accept), and an invalid input like -1 or 'abc' (reject with a validation message). Boundary value analysis (test the edges: min-1, min, max, max+1) and equivalence partitioning (group inputs that should behave the same and test one from each group) keep the case count small but powerful.

Functional testing covers positive paths (valid data, the happy path) and negative paths (invalid data, error handling). A login feature is not 'tested' until you have confirmed both that valid credentials log you in AND that wrong passwords, locked accounts, empty fields and SQL-like inputs are handled gracefully. Negative testing is where most real defects hide.

It operates at several levels. Unit-level functional tests check one function; integration-level checks that, for instance, the order service correctly calls the payment service; system-level checks a full user journey. End-to-end (E2E) functional testing follows a complete flow such as register, verify email, log in, add item to cart, pay, receive confirmation, and asserts the expected outcome at each step.

Functional testing answers 'does it work?', not 'how fast / how secure / how usable?'. A checkout that completes correctly but takes 30 seconds passes functional testing and fails performance testing. Keeping that boundary clear prevents you from logging the wrong kind of defect and helps you scope test runs accurately.
key_terms: requirement, acceptance criteria, positive testing, negative testing, boundary value analysis, equivalence partitioning, end-to-end testing, expected result
real_world_example: You receive a user story: 'As a shopper I can apply a coupon code at checkout; valid codes reduce the total, expired codes show an error.' You write functional cases: valid active code reduces total correctly; expired code shows 'This code has expired'; already-used one-time code is rejected; empty field does nothing; code with trailing spaces still works; case-insensitive code SAVE10 vs save10. You run each, compare the actual total and message to the expected, and log any mismatch as a functional defect.
step_by_step:
1. Read the requirement / user story and underline every testable rule.
2. List the inputs and identify their valid and invalid ranges.
3. Apply equivalence partitioning: group inputs that should behave identically.
4. Apply boundary value analysis: add cases at min-1, min, max, max+1 of each range.
5. Write positive cases (valid data, happy path) with explicit expected results.
6. Write negative cases (invalid data, empty fields, wrong format) with expected error handling.
7. Add at least one end-to-end case covering the full flow.
8. Execute, compare actual vs expected, and log mismatches with steps to reproduce.
common_mistakes:
Only testing the happy path and skipping negative cases where most bugs live.
Writing vague expected results like 'it works' instead of the exact value or message.
Forgetting boundary values (testing 18 but not 17 and 19 around an age rule).
Mixing in non-functional concerns (speed, layout) and logging them as functional bugs.
Testing against assumptions instead of the actual written requirement.
practical_use_case: Functional testing is the bread and butter of a manual QA's day: every new feature, every bug fix, and every acceptance check is fundamentally a functional test. You will write and execute functional test cases for nearly every ticket you touch.
summary: Functional testing verifies WHAT the system does against its requirements, using positive and negative cases derived with equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis. The reference is always the spec, and expected results must be concrete. Most real defects hide in negative paths and at boundaries.
example:
Functional test case (ready to paste into a test management tool):

ID: TC-LOGIN-003
Title: Reject login with correct email but wrong password
Priority: High
Preconditions: User account jane@example.com exists and is active; password is 'Correct123!'.
Steps:
1. Open the login page.
2. Enter email: jane@example.com
3. Enter password: Wrong999!
4. Click 'Log in'.
Expected result:
- User stays on the login page.
- An inline error 'Invalid email or password' is displayed.
- No session/auth cookie is set.
- After 5 consecutive failures the account is temporarily locked.
Actual result: <to be filled during execution>
Status: <Pass/Fail>
interactive: Requirement: 'A username must be 3 to 16 characters, letters and digits only.' Write five functional test cases (mix of positive and negative) including boundary values, each with input and expected result.
expected_answer: A strong answer uses boundary value analysis and includes both valid and invalid cases, for example: (1) 'abc' (3 chars) -> accepted (lower boundary). (2) 'ab' (2 chars) -> rejected, too short (min-1). (3) a 16-char alphanumeric string -> accepted (upper boundary). (4) a 17-char string -> rejected, too long (max+1). (5) 'john_doe' or 'jöhn' -> rejected, invalid character. Each case must state the exact input and the exact expected outcome (accept vs reject with the validation reason). Bonus: an empty input case and a digits-only case like '123'.
homework: Pick a real registration or signup form online. Write a functional test suite of at least 10 test cases covering positive, negative and boundary scenarios for at least three fields (e.g. email, password, age/date of birth). Use the TC template from the example.
expected_result: At least 10 well-formed test cases each with ID, title, preconditions, numbered steps and a specific expected result. The suite should demonstrate boundary value analysis and equivalence partitioning, include negative cases (empty, wrong format, too long/short) and at least one end-to-end 'successful registration' case. Expected results must be concrete (exact messages or states), not 'works fine'.

quiz.question: Functional testing primarily verifies:
answers: Whether the system does what the requirements say
Whether the code coverage is above 80%
How the UI looks on different screens

quiz.question: For a rule 'quantity must be between 1 and 99', which set best applies boundary value analysis?
Why is negative testing important in functional testing?
answers: Most real defects appear in error handling and invalid input
It replaces the need for positive tests

quiz.question: Which of these is a NON-functional concern, not a functional one?
answers: A valid coupon reduces the total
The checkout page loads within 3 seconds
An expired code shows an error message

title: Non-functional testing
short_description: How to verify the quality attributes of software such as performance, security, usability, reliability and compatibility.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
List the main non-functional quality attributes and what each measures.
Write a measurable non-functional requirement (e.g. p95 response time under 2s for 1000 users).
Choose the right non-functional test type (load, stress, security, usability) for a given concern.
Distinguish a functional defect from a non-functional defect when logging bugs.
theory:
Non-functional testing checks HOW WELL the system behaves rather than WHAT it does. The feature may work perfectly and still be unacceptable because it is too slow, insecure, unusable, or breaks on certain devices. These quality attributes are sometimes called the '-ilities': performance, scalability, reliability, availability, security, usability, maintainability, portability and compatibility.

Good non-functional requirements are measurable. 'The site should be fast' is useless; 'the search results page must return in under 2 seconds at the 95th percentile (p95) for 1000 concurrent users' is testable. Numbers, percentiles, user counts and durations turn a vague wish into a pass/fail criterion.

Key sub-types: Performance testing measures speed and resource use under expected load. Load testing applies expected peak load (e.g. 1000 users) and checks response times. Stress testing pushes beyond capacity (e.g. 5000 users) to find the breaking point and verify graceful failure. Security testing looks for vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, XSS, broken authentication and exposed data. Usability testing measures how easily real users accomplish tasks. Compatibility testing checks behavior across browsers, OSes and devices.

Non-functional testing often needs tools and environments closer to production. JMeter, k6 or Gatling generate load; OWASP ZAP or Burp Suite probe security; Lighthouse measures front-end performance and accessibility. Because results depend on hardware, network and data volume, these tests must be run in a representative environment, not a developer laptop.

When you find a problem, classify it correctly. A page that returns the wrong total is a functional bug; a page that returns the correct total but takes 12 seconds is a performance (non-functional) bug. Logging it under the right category routes it to the right owner and the right severity. Non-functional issues are often release-blockers even when every functional test passes.
key_terms: performance testing, load testing, stress testing, security testing, usability, scalability, response time (p95), reliability
real_world_example: Marketing announces a flash sale expected to draw 10x normal traffic. The features all work, but QA must confirm the site survives the spike. You build a load test in k6 simulating 2000 concurrent users hitting the product and checkout pages, and a stress test ramping to 6000 to find where it falls over. You discover the checkout API's p95 latency jumps from 800ms to 9s at 1500 users and the database connection pool exhausts. That is a non-functional defect that would have caused a costly outage during the sale.
step_by_step:
1. Identify the quality attribute at risk for this feature (speed? security? compatibility?).
2. Turn the concern into a measurable target (users, percentile, duration, threshold).
3. Choose the matching test type (load, stress, security, usability, compatibility).
4. Build a representative test environment and realistic test data volume.
5. Pick and configure the tool (e.g. k6/JMeter for load, ZAP for security, Lighthouse for front-end).
6. Establish a baseline measurement before the change.
7. Run the test, capture metrics (response times, error rate, throughput, resource use).
8. Compare against the target; log any breach as a non-functional defect with the metric and conditions.
common_mistakes:
Writing non-functional requirements without numbers ('should be fast/secure').
Running load tests on a laptop or tiny dataset, so results do not reflect production.
Reporting only average response time and hiding spikes; always use percentiles like p95/p99.
Confusing load testing (expected peak) with stress testing (beyond capacity).
Treating non-functional issues as low priority even when they would break the release.
practical_use_case: Before major releases, sales events, or scaling milestones, QA runs performance and security checks. Day to day you also flag non-functional concerns during functional testing (a slow page, a form that's hard to use, a layout that breaks on mobile) and route them to the right specialists.
summary: Non-functional testing checks HOW WELL the system performs across quality attributes like performance, security, usability and compatibility. Requirements must be measurable (users, percentiles, durations, thresholds) and run in representative environments with the right tools. A feature can pass every functional test and still be a release-blocker on a non-functional metric.
interactive: Rewrite this weak requirement into a measurable non-functional requirement and name which test type would verify it: 'The login should handle a lot of users and be secure.'
expected_answer: A strong answer splits it into two measurable NFRs and names the test types. Performance/load: 'The login endpoint must serve 2000 concurrent users with p95 response time under 1.5 seconds and error rate below 0.5% during a 15-minute peak' (verified by load testing with k6/JMeter). Security: 'The login must resist OWASP Top 10 attacks; specifically no SQL injection or credential brute-force without rate limiting/lockout after 5 attempts' (verified by security testing with OWASP ZAP and manual probing). The key is that each requirement now has numbers and a clear pass/fail, and is mapped to the correct test type.
homework: Choose a public website. Run Google Lighthouse (in Chrome DevTools) on its home page and one inner page. Capture the Performance, Accessibility and Best Practices scores plus the Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) values. Write a short report listing the three worst non-functional findings and which non-functional test type each relates to.
expected_result: A report containing actual Lighthouse scores and Core Web Vitals numbers (LCP in seconds, CLS value, performance score out of 100) for two pages, plus three concrete findings (e.g. 'LCP 5.4s, fails the 2.5s target -> performance', 'contrast issues on buttons -> usability/accessibility', 'missing security headers -> security'). Each finding should be tied to the correct non-functional category, not just listed.

quiz.question: Which is a NON-functional requirement?
answers: The dashboard loads within 2 seconds for 1000 concurrent users
The user can reset their password via email
An invalid coupon shows an error
The cart shows the correct item count

quiz.question: What is the difference between load testing and stress testing?
answers: Load tests expected peak load; stress tests beyond capacity to find the breaking point
Load testing is for security; stress testing is for speed
Stress testing only runs on mobile

quiz.question: Why should performance results use p95/p99 instead of only the average?
answers: p95 is always faster than the average
Percentiles reveal the slow-tail experience that averages hide
Averages cannot be measured by tools

quiz.question: A checkout returns the correct total but takes 11 seconds. This is best logged as:

title: Smoke testing
short_description: A quick, broad check that confirms a new build is stable enough to proceed with deeper testing.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Explain the purpose of smoke testing as a build-acceptance gate.
Build a smoke test suite covering the critical paths of an application.
Decide when to reject a build and send it back to development.
Distinguish smoke testing from sanity and regression testing.
theory:
Smoke testing (also called build verification testing) is a shallow but wide check run on every new build to answer one question: is this build stable enough to spend effort testing further? The term comes from hardware: power on the device and see if smoke comes out. In software, you power on the build and confirm the core functions don't immediately break.

It is broad and shallow: you touch many features but only at the surface. Typical smoke checks are 'the application launches', 'a user can log in', 'the home page loads', 'a key item can be added to cart', 'a record can be saved'. You are not testing edge cases or validation messages, you are confirming the major arteries are open.

Smoke testing acts as a gate. If a smoke test fails, the build is rejected and returned to development immediately, before the QA team wastes hours on detailed testing of something fundamentally broken. This saves time: there is no point running 300 regression cases on a build where login is dead.

It runs early and often, ideally on every build, and is a prime candidate for automation in CI/CD pipelines. An automated smoke suite of 10 to 20 critical checks can run in a couple of minutes after each deploy and immediately flag a broken build. Manual smoke tests are common too, especially right after a deployment to a new environment.

Smoke testing is scripted and predefined (you run the same critical-path checklist each time), unlike exploratory testing. It is broader and shallower than sanity testing, which is narrow and deep on one changed area. A useful mental model: smoke = 'is the whole thing alive?', sanity = 'does this one specific change work?'.
key_terms: build verification testing, build acceptance, critical path, gate / go-no-go, shallow and wide, CI/CD pipeline, build rejection, stability check
real_world_example: Every morning the CI pipeline deploys the latest build to the QA environment. Before the team starts detailed testing, you run the smoke suite: open the app, log in as a standard user, load the dashboard, create a new order, open the settings page, log out. Today 'create a new order' returns HTTP 500. You immediately mark the build as rejected, notify the dev team in Slack with the error and a screenshot, and the team avoids wasting the morning testing a broken build.
step_by_step:
1. Identify the application's critical paths (the few flows without which the product is useless).
2. Write one simple check per critical path with a clear expected result.
3. Keep each check shallow: valid data, happy path only.
4. Order them so a failure early (e.g. login) short-circuits the rest.
5. After each new build/deploy, run the full smoke suite first, before any deep testing.
6. If all pass, mark the build accepted and proceed to deeper testing.
7. If any critical check fails, reject the build, log the failure with evidence, and notify development.
8. Automate the suite in CI so it runs on every build.
common_mistakes:
Making the smoke suite too deep (adding edge cases and validations), which defeats the 'quick' purpose.
Making it too narrow so a broken core path slips through to detailed testing.
Continuing full testing even after a smoke test failed, wasting time on a dead build.
Confusing smoke (broad, every build) with sanity (narrow, after a specific change).
Not keeping the smoke suite updated as new critical features are added.
practical_use_case: Run after every deployment to a test or staging environment, and automatically in CI/CD after every build. It is the first thing a QA does each day with a fresh build and the gate that decides whether the team's testing effort can begin.
summary: Smoke testing is a broad, shallow check of an application's critical paths to confirm a build is stable enough to test further. It acts as a go/no-go gate: if it fails, the build is rejected before deeper testing wastes time. It is scripted, fast, run on every build, and a prime automation candidate in CI/CD.
example:
Smoke test checklist for an e-commerce web app (run on every new build):

[ ] 1. App URL loads, home page renders, no console errors (HTTP 200).
[ ] 2. Existing user can log in with valid credentials.
[ ] 3. Product listing page loads and shows products.
[ ] 4. A product can be opened (product detail page loads).
[ ] 5. A product can be added to the cart; cart count increases.
[ ] 6. Cart page opens and shows the added item.
[ ] 7. Checkout page loads (no payment needed for smoke).
[ ] 8. User can log out.

Decision rule: ALL must pass -> build accepted for deeper testing. ANY fails -> reject build, file a blocker bug, notify dev. Target run time: under 10 minutes manual / under 3 minutes automated.
interactive: For a simple note-taking app (login, create note, edit note, delete note, search notes, log out), write a 6-step smoke test checklist with the decision rule.
expected_answer: A strong answer lists 6 shallow, happy-path checks covering the critical paths: (1) app loads / login page appears, (2) user logs in with valid credentials, (3) create a new note and it appears in the list, (4) open and edit an existing note, save succeeds, (5) delete a note and it disappears, (6) log out returns to login screen. It should explicitly state the decision rule: if all pass the build is accepted for deeper testing; if any fail the build is rejected and sent back to development. The checks should be broad and shallow (no validation edge cases), and ideally ordered so login failure stops the rest.
homework: Pick a web or mobile app you use. Identify its critical paths and write a complete smoke test suite (8 to 12 checks) with expected results and a clear accept/reject decision rule. Then note which of these you would automate first and why.
expected_result: A smoke suite of 8 to 12 broad, shallow, happy-path checks covering only the application's critical paths, each with an expected result, plus an explicit go/no-go decision rule. The submission should justify automation priorities (e.g. login and home-page load first because they gate everything else) and should NOT include deep edge-case or validation testing, demonstrating the student understands smoke testing's scope.

quiz.question: The main purpose of smoke testing is to:
answers: Decide whether a build is stable enough for deeper testing
Find every bug in the build

quiz.question: Smoke testing is best described as:
If a smoke test fails, the correct action is usually to:
answers: Reject the build and return it to development
Convert it into a usability test

quiz.question: Why is smoke testing a good candidate for CI/CD automation?
answers: It is a small, repeatable critical-path check that can run on every build in minutes
It only runs once per release

title: Sanity testing
short_description: A narrow, focused check that a specific change or bug fix works as intended before broader testing continues.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Explain when sanity testing is used and how it differs from smoke and regression testing.
Scope a sanity check tightly around a single change or fix.
Decide whether the build deserves fuller testing based on the sanity result.
Write a focused sanity test for a given bug fix or minor change.
theory:
Sanity testing is a narrow and deep check performed after a small change, a bug fix, or a minor enhancement, to confirm that the specific functionality now works and that the related logic is rational ('sane') before investing in fuller testing. If smoke testing asks 'is the whole build alive?', sanity testing asks 'does this particular change actually work, and does the area around it still make sense?'.

It is usually unscripted or lightly scripted and done quickly. After developers deliver a fix for, say, a tax-calculation bug, you don't re-run the whole regression suite first, you sanity-check the tax calculation across a few representative cases. If those fail, there is no point doing more, the fix is wrong and the build goes back.

Sanity testing is a subset of regression testing in spirit but tightly scoped. It targets only the changed area and its immediate neighbors, not the entire application. Because it is focused, it is fast and acts as a second, narrower gate after smoke testing: smoke confirms the build is stable overall; sanity confirms the specific change is worth deeper testing.

A practical sequence on a fix build: run smoke (is the app alive?), then sanity (does the fix work and is the surrounding logic reasonable?), then, only if both pass, run targeted regression and retesting. This staged gating prevents wasted effort on builds that fail at the cheapest possible check.

Keep the distinctions crisp: smoke = broad and shallow across the whole build, run on every build, often automated. Sanity = narrow and deep on one changed area, run after specific changes, often manual and unscripted. Regression = re-running existing tests broadly to catch side effects. Confusing these in interviews and on the job is a classic beginner error.
key_terms: narrow and deep, change verification, rationality check, subset of regression, unscripted, gating, scope, bug-fix verification
real_world_example: Developers ship a build that fixes a bug where discount percentages over 50% were not applied. You don't immediately launch full regression. You sanity-test the discount logic: apply a 10% code, a 50% code, a 75% code and a 100% code, and confirm each reduces the total correctly and the math is rational (75% off a $100 item = $25). The 75% case now works but the 100% code makes the total negative. The fix is incomplete, so you reject the build after a five-minute sanity check instead of running the whole suite.
step_by_step:
1. Read exactly what changed (the ticket, the fix, the affected function).
2. Identify the narrow area and its immediate neighbors that the change could affect.
3. Pick a few representative inputs that exercise that specific logic, including the previously broken case.
4. Run smoke first to confirm the build is generally stable.
5. Execute the focused sanity checks on the changed area.
6. Judge whether the results are not just passing but rational/sensible.
7. If sane, proceed to fuller regression and retesting; if not, reject the build with evidence.
common_mistakes:
Confusing sanity with smoke (sanity is narrow/deep on a change; smoke is broad/shallow on the whole build).
Broadening a sanity check into a full regression, losing the speed benefit.
Only checking the previously broken value and ignoring nearby cases (e.g. fixing 75% but missing 100%).
Skipping the 'is the result rational?' judgment and just checking it doesn't crash.
Running sanity before confirming the build even boots (skipping smoke).
practical_use_case: Used many times a day whenever developers deliver a small fix or minor change: you quickly confirm the change works and is sensible before deciding to invest in deeper regression. It keeps QA effort proportional to the size and risk of the change.
summary: Sanity testing is a narrow, deep check of a specific change or bug fix, plus its immediate neighbors, to confirm it works and the surrounding logic is rational before deeper testing. It is a second gate after smoke and is tightly scoped, unlike full regression. Remember: smoke = is the whole build alive; sanity = does this one change work.
example:
Sanity test note for bug fix JIRA-902 'Discount over 50% not applied':

Scope: Discount calculation only (changed module). Immediate neighbors: cart total, tax-after-discount.
Build: 2.14.3-rc2
Smoke result: PASS (app loads, login OK, cart works).
Sanity cases:
1. 10% code on $100 item -> expected total $90.00 -> Actual $90.00 -> PASS
2. 50% code on $100 item -> expected $50.00 -> Actual $50.00 -> PASS
3. 75% code on $100 item (the fixed case) -> expected $25.00 -> Actual $25.00 -> PASS
4. 100% code on $100 item -> expected $0.00 -> Actual -$0.00 / shows negative -> FAIL
5. Tax recalculated on discounted price? -> expected yes -> Actual yes -> PASS
Decision: Build REJECTED. Fix incomplete: boundary at 100% produces invalid total. Returned to dev with case 4 evidence.
interactive: A bug 'search returns no results when the query has trailing spaces' has just been fixed. Write a tight sanity test (4 focused checks plus a decision rule) for this fix.
expected_answer: A strong answer stays narrow and deep on search-with-spaces and immediate neighbors, for example: (1) search 'shoes ' with a trailing space -> returns the same results as 'shoes' (the fixed case). (2) search ' shoes' with a leading space -> returns correct results. (3) search 'shoes' with no spaces -> still works (no regression in the normal case). (4) search '  ' (only spaces) -> returns an empty/no-results state gracefully, not an error. Decision rule: if all are correct and the results are rational, proceed to fuller regression; if any fail, reject the build. The answer should NOT expand into testing the whole app, and should include the previously broken case plus a rationality judgment.
homework: Find a recent bug fix in any app (or invent a realistic one, e.g. 'dates show in the wrong format after editing'). Write a focused sanity test plan: state the scope, list 4 to 6 narrow checks including the previously broken case and immediate neighbors, and give a clear go/no-go decision rule. Explicitly explain how this differs from a smoke test of the same app.
expected_result: A tightly scoped sanity plan covering only the changed area and its immediate neighbors, including the previously broken case, with concrete expected results and a go/no-go rule. The submission must clearly contrast sanity (narrow/deep on the change) with smoke (broad/shallow on the whole build) for the chosen app, demonstrating the student does not conflate the two.

quiz.question: Sanity testing is best described as:
answers: Narrow and deep on a specific change
Broad and shallow across the whole build
A full regression of every feature

quiz.question: Sanity testing is usually performed:
answers: After a small change or bug fix, to confirm it works before deeper testing
Only at the very end of the project

quiz.question: Which sequence is most sensible for a fix build?
answers: Smoke, then sanity, then targeted regression/retesting

quiz.question: A key difference between smoke and sanity testing is:
answers: Smoke is narrow/deep; sanity is broad/shallow
Smoke is manual; sanity is always automated
Smoke checks the whole build is alive; sanity checks one specific change is correct

title: Regression testing
short_description: Re-running existing tests after a change to confirm that previously working functionality still works.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Explain why changes cause regressions and how regression testing catches them.
Select which test cases to include in a regression suite using risk and impact analysis.
Decide between full and partial (selective) regression for a given change.
Describe how regression suites are automated and maintained over time.
theory:
Regression testing re-runs existing test cases after any change (new feature, bug fix, configuration update, library upgrade) to confirm that functionality that used to work still works. A 'regression' is a defect where something previously correct becomes broken as an unintended side effect of a change. Because code is interconnected, fixing or adding one thing can silently break another, regression testing is the safety net.

Unlike retesting, which re-runs the one specific failing test for a fixed bug, regression testing is broad: it re-verifies a body of existing, passing tests around and beyond the change. The challenge is scope. Running every test on every change is expensive, so QA uses test selection: full regression for high-risk or wide-reaching changes (major release, framework upgrade), and selective/partial regression for small, localized changes, choosing tests by impact analysis (which areas does this change touch?).

Prioritization matters. A regression suite is typically prioritized by risk and business criticality: core flows (login, checkout, payment) are always included; rarely used, low-impact features may be cycled in less often. Techniques include regression test selection (run only affected tests), test case prioritization (run the highest-risk first), and suite minimization (remove redundant cases).

Regression testing is the number-one driver of test automation. Because the same cases run again and again across releases, automating them (Selenium, Cypress, Playwright, pytest) pays off quickly: an automated regression suite can run overnight or in CI on every merge, catching side-effects within minutes instead of days. Manual regression still exists but does not scale for large, frequently changing products.

A regression suite is a living asset. It must be maintained: add cases for every new feature and every fixed bug (so the bug can never silently return), update cases when requirements change, and remove obsolete ones. A stale regression suite gives false confidence; a well-maintained one is one of the most valuable things a QA team owns.
key_terms: regression, side effect, impact analysis, test selection, full vs partial regression, test case prioritization, regression suite, suite maintenance
real_world_example: Your team upgrades the payment library from v3 to v4. The new feature works, but this change touches every checkout flow. You run a full regression of the payment and checkout suite: credit card, PayPal, saved cards, refunds, partial refunds, currency conversion, failed payments. You discover that refunds now round incorrectly to the nearest dollar, a regression introduced by the upgrade, even though nobody touched the refund code directly. Without regression testing this would have shipped to production and caused accounting errors.
step_by_step:
1. Identify exactly what changed and which modules/features it could touch (impact analysis).
2. Decide scope: full regression (high-risk/wide change) or selective (localized change).
3. Pull the relevant existing test cases from the regression suite.
4. Prioritize: always include core/critical flows; order high-risk cases first.
5. Execute the selected cases (manually or via automation), recording pass/fail.
6. Investigate every failure to determine if it is a genuine regression or a test that needs updating.
7. Log genuine regressions as defects with the change that likely caused them.
8. After the fix, add or update cases so the regression suite stays current.
common_mistakes:
Running only the new feature's tests and forgetting that the change can break unrelated areas.
Always doing full regression even for tiny changes, wasting time; or always doing tiny selective regression for risky changes, missing side effects.
Not maintaining the suite, so it tests obsolete behavior and misses new features.
Failing to add a regression test for each fixed bug, allowing bugs to silently return.
Treating every failed regression case as a product bug without checking if the test itself is outdated.
practical_use_case: Run before every release, after every significant merge, and after dependency or environment changes. For most teams an automated regression suite runs continuously in CI, while QA performs targeted manual regression on areas automation does not yet cover.
summary: Regression testing re-runs existing tests after a change to catch side effects that break previously working functionality. Scope is chosen via impact analysis (full vs selective) and prioritized by risk, with core flows always included. It is the main driver of automation and must be maintained continuously, adding a case for every new feature and every fixed bug.
example:
Selective regression plan for change 'Add gift-wrapping option to checkout':

Impact analysis: change touches the cart total, order summary, checkout submission and order confirmation email. Unaffected: login, product search, user profile.

Regression scope: PARTIAL (localized to checkout).

Selected existing cases (re-run, must still pass):
1. TC-CART-01 Add item, total correct without gift wrap.
2. TC-CHK-04 Apply coupon, discounted total correct.
3. TC-CHK-07 Pay with credit card, order created.
4. TC-CHK-09 Tax calculated correctly on order total.
5. TC-ORD-02 Confirmation email contains correct line items and total.
6. TC-CHK-12 Empty cart cannot proceed to checkout.

Priority order: payment (TC-CHK-07) and tax (TC-CHK-09) first (highest business risk).
New case to ADD to suite: TC-CHK-15 Gift wrap adds correct fee to total.
Result: all 6 must pass to confirm no regression; failures logged as defects tied to the gift-wrap change.
interactive: A change 'allow users to update their email address in profile settings' has just merged. Do an impact analysis and list 5 existing test cases you would re-run as regression, with a one-line reason each, and state whether you'd do full or partial regression.
expected_answer: A strong answer first states scope (partial/selective, since the change is localized to profile/auth) and does impact analysis: changing email affects login, password reset, notification emails and account uniqueness. It then lists ~5 existing cases with reasons, e.g.: (1) login with the new email still works; (2) login with the OLD email no longer works; (3) password-reset email is sent to the new address; (4) email-uniqueness validation still rejects an address already in use; (5) account/profile page displays the updated email. Each reason ties to a possible side effect of the change. The answer recognizes that even a 'profile' change ripples into authentication, which is the core insight of regression testing.
homework: For an application you know, design a prioritized regression suite of 12 to 15 test cases covering its critical flows. Mark each case High/Medium/Low priority with a justification, indicate which would be in a 'smoke + critical' subset run on every build, and describe how you would maintain the suite as the product evolves.
expected_result: A prioritized regression suite of 12 to 15 cases covering the app's critical flows, each with a priority and justification based on risk/business impact, plus a clearly identified critical subset for frequent runs. The submission should explain a maintenance strategy (add a case per fixed bug and per new feature, update on requirement changes, remove obsolete cases) and ideally note which cases are good automation candidates.

quiz.question: A regression is:
answers: Previously working functionality that breaks as a side effect of a change

quiz.question: Selective (partial) regression means:
answers: Running every test in the suite
Running only the tests affected by the change, chosen via impact analysis

quiz.question: Why is regression testing the strongest driver of automation?
answers: The same cases must be re-run repeatedly across releases

quiz.question: After a bug is fixed, what should be added to keep it from silently returning?
answers: A regression test case covering that bug

title: Retesting
short_description: Re-executing the exact failed test case after a defect is fixed to confirm the specific bug is gone.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Define retesting (confirmation testing) and its purpose.
Clearly distinguish retesting from regression testing.
Use a bug report's reproduction steps to retest a fix correctly.
Decide what to do when a retest passes, fails, or partially passes.
theory:
Retesting, also called confirmation testing, is re-running the exact test case that previously failed, after developers report the underlying defect as fixed, to confirm the bug is actually gone. It is narrow and specific: you reproduce the original failure conditions and verify the new, correct behavior, using the steps recorded in the bug report.

The critical distinction every QA must master: retesting targets the specific fixed defect, while regression testing targets surrounding functionality to catch new side effects. Retesting asks 'is bug JIRA-1234 fixed?'; regression asks 'did fixing JIRA-1234 break anything else?'. In practice you usually do both after a fix: retest the bug, then run regression around it.

Retesting requires the original defect to have a clear, reproducible test case. This is why good bug reports matter: precise reproduction steps, environment, build, expected vs actual result. To retest, you take those exact steps on the new build where the fix landed and check that the actual result now matches the expected result. If the steps were vague, retesting becomes guesswork.

Retesting is not a good automation candidate by itself, because it is tied to a single, often one-off defect verification on a specific build. (Once confirmed fixed, the case is typically promoted into the regression suite, which IS automated, so the bug can never silently return.) So retesting is usually manual and quick.

Outcomes drive next steps. If the retest passes, you close the defect and ideally add it to the regression suite. If it fails, you reopen the defect with new evidence and return it to development. If it partially passes (the main case works but a related boundary still fails, like the 100%-discount example), you document precisely what still fails so the developer can finish the fix.
key_terms: confirmation testing, defect verification, reproduction steps, fixed build, reopen / close, expected vs actual, bug lifecycle, regression promotion
real_world_example: Bug JIRA-1234 says 'uploading a PDF over 10MB shows a blank error with no message'. A developer marks it fixed in build 4.2.1. You open the bug, follow its exact steps: log in, go to Documents, upload a 12MB PDF. Now the system shows a clear message 'File exceeds the 10MB limit'. The actual result matches the expected result, so you retest as PASS, close JIRA-1234, and add this case to the regression suite so the validation can never regress. Had it still failed, you would reopen with a screenshot and the build number.
step_by_step:
1. Confirm the defect is marked 'Fixed/Ready for QA' and note the build that contains the fix.
2. Open the original bug report and read its exact reproduction steps, expected and actual results.
3. Set up the same environment, data and preconditions described in the report.
4. Execute the exact steps on the fixed build, no improvising.
5. Compare the new actual result to the expected result in the report.
6. If it matches: mark retest PASS, close the defect, and promote the case into the regression suite.
7. If it does not match: mark FAIL, reopen the defect with new evidence (screenshot, build, steps).
8. If partially fixed: document precisely which part still fails and reopen with that detail.
common_mistakes:
Confusing retesting with regression (retesting = the one fixed bug; regression = everything around it).
Not following the exact original reproduction steps, so the test doesn't actually exercise the bug.
Retesting on the wrong build (one that doesn't contain the fix yet).
Closing the defect on a partial fix without documenting what still fails.
Forgetting to add the confirmed-fixed case to the regression suite.
practical_use_case: Done continuously throughout a sprint: every time a developer marks a bug as fixed, the QA retests it to confirm before closing. It is a core part of the bug lifecycle and one of the most frequent QA activities day to day.
summary: Retesting (confirmation testing) re-runs the exact failed case on the build containing the fix to confirm a specific bug is gone. It is narrow and tied to one defect, unlike broad regression, and depends on clear reproduction steps in the bug report. A pass closes the defect and the case is promoted into the regression suite so the bug can never silently return.
example:
Retest record for a fixed defect:

Defect: JIRA-1234 'Upload of PDF > 10MB shows blank error, no message'
Fix delivered in build: 4.2.1
Environment: QA, Chrome 124, Windows 11, user role: Standard
Original reproduction steps:
1. Log in as a standard user.
2. Navigate to Documents > Upload.
3. Select a 12MB PDF file.
4. Click Upload.
Original actual result: blank red box, no text, file not uploaded.
Expected result: clear message 'File exceeds the 10MB limit. Please upload a smaller file.'

Retest on build 4.2.1:
Actual result: message 'File exceeds the 10MB limit. Please upload a smaller file.' is shown; upload blocked.
Match expected? YES
Retest verdict: PASS -> Defect CLOSED.
Follow-up: Add as regression case TC-DOC-21 (oversized file shows correct error). Run regression on the upload area to confirm valid uploads (e.g. 2MB PDF) still work.
interactive: A bug 'clicking Save twice quickly creates two duplicate records' is marked fixed in build 5.0.3. Describe exactly how you would retest it, including what a PASS, a FAIL, and a partial-pass would look like.
expected_answer: A strong answer reproduces the original conditions on build 5.0.3: open a new record form, fill it in, then click Save twice in rapid succession (the exact trigger from the bug). Expected result: only ONE record is created (the second click is ignored/disabled). PASS = exactly one record exists after the double click, on the build containing the fix. FAIL = two records still created -> reopen the defect with the build number and a screenshot of both duplicates. Partial pass = the duplicate is prevented for fast double-clicks but a slower second click (e.g. after the button re-enables) still creates a duplicate -> document the precise remaining failure and reopen. The answer should also mention adding the confirmed case to the regression suite once it passes, and running regression on the save flow.
homework: Take three bug reports (real ones from a tracker you can access, or write three realistic ones with full reproduction steps). For each, write a complete retest plan: the build that must contain the fix, the exact steps, the expected result, and the decision rule for pass/fail/partial. Then explain for each how retesting differs from the regression you would also run.
expected_result: Three retest plans, each tied to a specific defect with a build number, exact reproduction steps copied from the report, a concrete expected result, and a clear pass/fail/partial decision rule. Each plan must clearly distinguish the narrow retest (verifying that one bug) from the broader regression run around it, and should mention promoting confirmed fixes into the regression suite.

quiz.question: Retesting (confirmation testing) means:
answers: Re-running all tests in the project
Re-running the exact failed case to confirm a specific bug is fixed

quiz.question: The key difference between retesting and regression testing is:
answers: Retesting checks the specific fixed bug; regression checks for new side effects elsewhere
Retesting is always automated; regression is always manual

quiz.question: What makes a defect easy to retest reliably?
answers: Clear, reproducible steps with expected vs actual results in the bug report

quiz.question: After a retest passes, a good practice is to:
answers: Promote the case into the regression suite so the bug can't silently return

title: Exploratory testing
short_description: Simultaneously learning, designing and executing tests without predefined scripts to uncover defects scripts miss.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Explain exploratory testing and when it outperforms scripted testing.
Run a time-boxed exploratory session using charters and the session-based model.
Apply heuristics (like SFDPOT and CRUD) to generate test ideas on the fly.
Document findings and reproduction steps from an unscripted session.
theory:
Exploratory testing is an approach where the tester simultaneously learns the product, designs tests, and executes them, using the results of each action to decide the next. There is no detailed pre-written script; instead the tester uses experience, intuition and heuristics to probe the application. It is structured improvisation, not random clicking.

It shines where scripts are weak: brand-new features with thin requirements, complex workflows with many states, and areas where you want to find the 'unknown unknowns', the bugs nobody thought to write a case for. Scripted testing confirms expected behavior; exploratory testing discovers unexpected behavior. The two are complementary, most teams use both.

To keep it disciplined, professionals use session-based test management (SBTM): time-boxed sessions (typically 60 to 120 minutes) driven by a charter, a short mission like 'explore the file-upload feature focusing on file types and size limits to discover validation gaps'. The tester takes notes throughout, recording what was tested, bugs found, questions raised, and areas not covered.

Heuristics generate ideas quickly. SFDPOT (Structure, Function, Data, Platform, Operations, Time) prompts you to vary inputs along different dimensions. CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) ensures you exercise each data operation. 'Goldilocks' (too big, too small, just right) and boundary thinking surface edge cases. Tours (e.g. the 'money tour' for revenue paths, the 'back-button tour') give you angles of attack.

Good exploratory testing is documented and reproducible. When you find a bug while improvising, you must be able to reconstruct the steps, so note your path as you go, capture screenshots and network logs, and after the session write a clear bug report with reproduction steps. The output of a session is typically a short session report plus any defects, not a pass/fail checklist.
key_terms: session-based test management, charter, time-boxing, heuristics, SFDPOT, tours, test idea, simultaneous learning and execution
real_world_example: A new 'bulk import contacts from CSV' feature ships with only a one-line requirement. There are no detailed cases yet. You run a 90-minute charter: 'Explore CSV import focusing on malformed files, encodings and duplicates'. Improvising, you try a file with 50,000 rows (it times out at HTTP 504), a file with emojis in names (they become question marks), a CSV with a missing header (it silently drops the first contact), and duplicate emails (some imported twice). None of these were in any script, yet you found four real defects in 90 minutes by exploring along data and boundary heuristics.
step_by_step:
1. Pick a focused charter: what area, what risk, what mission, for how long (time-box it).
2. Spend the first minutes learning the feature and forming a mental model.
3. Choose a heuristic to drive ideas (SFDPOT, CRUD, boundaries, tours).
4. Execute test ideas, letting each result suggest the next probe.
5. Take running notes: path taken, observations, bugs, questions, untested areas.
6. Capture evidence as you go (screenshots, console/network logs) so bugs are reproducible.
7. When the time-box ends, stop and write a short session report.
8. Turn each finding into a proper bug report with exact reproduction steps, and feed strong cases into the scripted suite.
common_mistakes:
Treating exploratory testing as random clicking with no charter, focus, or notes.
Not documenting the path, so a found bug cannot be reproduced.
Letting sessions run unbounded instead of time-boxing them.
Using it as a replacement for scripted/regression testing rather than a complement.
Ignoring heuristics and only repeating the same obvious happy-path actions.
practical_use_case: Used for new features before scripted cases exist, for risk-based testing of complex areas, during bug hunts before a release, and whenever requirements are vague. Many teams schedule regular exploratory sessions alongside their automated regression suite.
summary: Exploratory testing is disciplined improvisation: you simultaneously learn, design and execute tests, guided by a charter, a time-box and heuristics like SFDPOT and CRUD. It excels at finding the unexpected defects scripts miss, especially in new or under-specified features. The output is a documented session report plus reproducible bug reports, not random clicking.
example:
Session-based exploratory test report:

Charter: Explore the password-reset flow focusing on email handling, token expiry and reuse, to find security/validation gaps.
Time-box: 60 minutes. Tester: A. Polo. Build: 3.7.0. Environment: QA, Chrome 124.
Heuristics used: CRUD on tokens, boundary on expiry time, 'money/security tour'.

Notes / path:
- Requested reset for valid email -> email received in ~5s. OK.
- Requested reset for non-existent email -> message reveals 'no account found' (info leak). BUG-1.
- Used the reset link twice -> second use still resets the password (token not single-use). BUG-2.
- Waited 65 min then used link -> link still works (token says it expires in 60 min). BUG-3.
- Reset with password 'a' (1 char) -> accepted, no strength validation. BUG-4.

Defects raised: BUG-1 (account enumeration), BUG-2 (reusable token), BUG-3 (expiry not enforced), BUG-4 (weak password accepted).
Areas not covered (for next session): rate limiting on reset requests, email link on mobile.
Coverage: ~70% of the reset flow explored.
interactive: Write an exploratory testing charter (mission + focus + time-box) for the search feature of an e-commerce site, then list six test ideas you would try, each tagged with the heuristic that generated it.
expected_answer: A strong answer gives a focused, time-boxed charter, e.g. 'Explore product search for 60 minutes focusing on special characters, empty/extreme inputs and result relevance to find input-handling and edge-case defects.' Then six ideas, each tied to a heuristic: (1) search empty string -> what happens? (boundary/Goldilocks). (2) search a 500-character query (boundary/too big). (3) search with special chars like %, ', <script> (data/security). (4) search a term with no matches -> graceful empty state? (function). (5) search, then hit back button mid-load (operations/back-button tour). (6) search the same term on mobile vs desktop (platform/SFDPOT). The answer should show that ideas are generated systematically from heuristics, not at random, and the charter is bounded in time and scope.
homework: Run a real 45 to 60 minute session-based exploratory test on any web or mobile app, driven by a written charter. Produce a session report containing: the charter, heuristics used, your running notes, at least three findings (bugs or questions) with reproduction steps and evidence, areas not covered, and a rough coverage estimate.
expected_result: A complete session report with a focused, time-boxed charter, named heuristics, a real running log of the path taken, and at least three findings written as reproducible bug reports (steps, expected vs actual, screenshots). It should list untested areas and a coverage estimate, demonstrating disciplined, documented exploration rather than unstructured clicking.

quiz.question: Exploratory testing is best described as:
answers: Simultaneously learning, designing and executing tests, guided by heuristics and a charter

quiz.question: A charter in session-based test management is:
answers: A short mission stating the area, focus and goal of the session
A list of all bugs in the product

quiz.question: Exploratory testing is especially valuable when:
answers: Requirements are thin or the feature is new and full of unknowns
You only need to re-confirm a fixed bug

quiz.question: Which is essential so an exploratory finding can be acted on?
answers: Documented path and reproduction steps with evidence

title: Usability testing
short_description: Evaluating how easily and satisfyingly real users can accomplish tasks with the product.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Distinguish usability testing (real users doing tasks) from usability/heuristic evaluation (expert review).
Write task-based usability test scenarios and success criteria.
Measure usability with concrete metrics (task success rate, time on task, error rate, SUS score).
Run a moderated session and report findings with severity ratings.
theory:
Usability testing evaluates how easy, efficient and satisfying a product is to use, by observing real users attempting realistic tasks. It targets effectiveness (can users complete the task?), efficiency (how quickly and with how much effort?) and satisfaction (how do they feel about it?). It answers 'can people actually use this?', a question functional testing never asks.

There are two related practices. Usability testing proper uses representative end users performing tasks while you observe. Heuristic (expert) evaluation has specialists review the interface against principles like Nielsen's 10 heuristics (visibility of system status, error prevention, consistency, match with the real world, etc.). The first finds what real users struggle with; the second is cheaper and catches obvious violations early. QA often does lightweight heuristic checks; dedicated UX research runs full user studies.

Good usability tests are task-based, not opinion-based. Instead of asking 'do you like this page?', you give a task: 'You want to return an item you bought last week, please do that.' Then you watch where users hesitate, click the wrong thing, or give up. Success criteria are defined in advance (e.g. user completes the return in under 3 minutes without help).

Usability is measurable. Common metrics: task success rate (% who complete the task), time on task, error rate (wrong clicks/dead ends), and the System Usability Scale (SUS), a standardized 10-item questionnaire producing a 0 to 100 score (above ~68 is considered above average). Combining quantitative metrics with qualitative observations ('three of five users couldn't find the return button') gives actionable findings.

Logistics: as few as 5 users typically surface the majority of major usability problems (the classic Nielsen finding), so studies don't need huge samples. Sessions can be moderated (a facilitator guides and probes) or unmoderated (users record themselves via a tool). Findings are reported with severity ratings so the team fixes the worst blockers first, like a 'submit' button hidden below the fold that prevents purchases.
key_terms: task-based testing, task success rate, time on task, System Usability Scale (SUS), heuristic evaluation, Nielsen's heuristics, moderated vs unmoderated, severity rating
real_world_example: A bank redesigns its money-transfer screen. Functionally everything works, but support tickets spike. You run a moderated usability test with 5 customers, each given the task 'transfer $50 to a saved payee'. Four of five hesitate because the 'Confirm' button is grey and looks disabled, and two enter the amount in the wrong field because the labels are ambiguous. Task success drops to 60% with an average time on task of 4 minutes against a 1-minute target. These are usability defects, the feature works but real users can't use it efficiently, and you report them with high severity.
step_by_step:
1. Define the goal: which flows and which user group are you evaluating?
2. Recruit ~5 representative users (or run a heuristic evaluation if users aren't available).
3. Write 3 to 5 realistic, task-based scenarios with predefined success criteria.
4. Decide metrics to capture: success rate, time on task, errors, post-task SUS.
5. Run sessions: give one task at a time, observe silently, encourage think-aloud, do not coach.
6. Record where users hesitate, err, or fail, and capture their comments.
7. Aggregate metrics and group observations into findings.
8. Rate each finding by severity and report with concrete recommendations.
common_mistakes:
Asking for opinions ('do you like it?') instead of giving tasks and observing behavior.
Leading or coaching the user during the task, which hides the real friction.
Testing with teammates or yourself, who already know the product, instead of representative users.
Reporting only feelings with no metrics (success rate, time on task) to back them up.
Confusing usability testing (real users) with heuristic evaluation (expert review).
practical_use_case: Run when launching a new flow or redesign, when support/complaints suggest people are confused, or before major UX changes. QA frequently flags usability issues during functional testing and may run lightweight heuristic checks even without a formal study.
summary: Usability testing observes real users completing realistic tasks to measure effectiveness, efficiency and satisfaction. It is task-based, not opinion-based, and measured with metrics like task success rate, time on task and the SUS score, with findings rated by severity. As few as five users surface most major usability problems.
example:
Usability test plan and finding (filled in):

Goal: Evaluate the checkout redesign for first-time shoppers.
Participants: 5 representative users (online shoppers, not employees).
Task 1: 'Buy the blue running shoes in size 9 and pay with a credit card.'
  Success criterion: order placed in under 2 minutes without assistance.
Metrics: task success rate, time on task, errors, SUS at the end.

Results:
  Task success rate: 3/5 (60%).
  Avg time on task: 3m 20s (target 2m).
  Common errors: 4/5 missed the size dropdown (looked like static text); 2/5 clicked 'Continue shopping' thinking it was 'Continue to payment'.
  SUS score: 58 (below the ~68 average).

Finding F-1 (Severity: High): The size selector is not recognizable as interactive; 4/5 users failed to select a size on the first try.
Recommendation: Style the size selector as an obvious dropdown/segmented control with a clear label and placeholder 'Select size'.
interactive: Write three task-based usability scenarios (with success criteria) for a food-delivery app, and list which metrics you'd capture for each. Then turn one likely problem into a finding with a severity rating.
expected_answer: A strong answer gives realistic tasks, not opinions, e.g.: (1) 'Order a large pepperoni pizza for delivery to your saved address' (success: order placed under 3 min, no help). (2) 'Apply the promo code WELCOME10 to your order' (success: discount visible before payment). (3) 'Track your most recent order's status' (success: user finds the live tracking screen). Metrics: task success rate, time on task, error count, and a post-test SUS score. A sample finding: 'Severity High, F-1: 3/5 users could not locate where to enter the promo code because it is hidden under a collapsed "Have a code?" link; recommend surfacing the field on the payment screen.' The answer should use observation/metrics rather than asking whether users 'like' the app.
homework: Pick an app or website and recruit 2 to 3 people who have never used it. Give each the same 3 task-based scenarios, observe silently, and record task success, time on task, and the points where they struggled. Compile a short usability report with metrics, at least three findings rated by severity, and one concrete recommendation per finding.
expected_result: A usability report based on real observation of 2 to 3 representative users performing predefined tasks, including quantitative metrics (success rate, time on task, errors) and at least three severity-rated findings each with a concrete recommendation. It should show the student observed behavior without coaching and avoided opinion-only ('do you like it') questions, distinguishing usability testing from a satisfaction survey.

quiz.question: Usability testing primarily measures:
answers: How easily and satisfyingly real users can complete tasks

quiz.question: Which is a proper usability task rather than an opinion question?
The System Usability Scale (SUS) produces:
answers: A standardized 0 to 100 usability score (around 68 is average)

quiz.question: Roughly how many users typically surface the majority of major usability problems?

title: Compatibility testing
short_description: Verifying that software works correctly across different browsers, operating systems, devices, networks and configurations.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Explain what compatibility testing covers and why fragmentation makes it necessary.
Build a target matrix of browsers, OS versions, devices and resolutions.
Use a risk/usage-based strategy to prioritize which combinations to test.
Distinguish forward and backward compatibility and apply each.
theory:
Compatibility testing checks that an application behaves correctly across the many environments users actually run it in: different browsers and versions, operating systems, devices, screen sizes and resolutions, network speeds, and sometimes databases, hardware and integrated software. It is a non-functional type because the feature 'works', but it may work only on the developer's machine and break elsewhere.

The driver is fragmentation. Users run Chrome, Safari, Firefox and Edge; Windows, macOS, Android and iOS; phones, tablets and desktops; old and new versions of each. A layout that is perfect on Chrome 124 / Windows can be broken on Safari / iOS because of CSS differences, or a date picker can fail on an older Android WebView. Compatibility testing surfaces these environment-specific defects before users do.

It is structured around a compatibility matrix: a table of the target combinations (e.g. browser x OS x device). Because the full matrix is huge (dozens of browsers times OS versions times devices is thousands of combinations), you prioritize by real usage data and risk. Pull your actual audience's browser/OS/device share from analytics and cover the top combinations that represent, say, 90%+ of traffic, plus any known-risky edge (oldest supported version).

Two important directions: backward compatibility (does the app still work on older versions, e.g. the oldest browser/OS you officially support?) and forward compatibility (will it keep working on newer, upcoming versions, e.g. a beta browser release?). You also distinguish hardware/device compatibility, OS compatibility, browser compatibility and network compatibility (3G vs Wi-Fi).

Execution mixes real devices and emulation. Cloud device farms (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs, LambdaTest) provide hundreds of real browser/OS/device combos on demand; emulators/simulators and responsive-mode in DevTools are cheaper for quick checks but less accurate for things like touch, GPU rendering and real network behavior. A good strategy tests critical flows on real top-priority devices and uses emulation for breadth.
key_terms: compatibility matrix, fragmentation, backward compatibility, forward compatibility, browser/OS/device coverage, device farm, emulator vs real device, resolution/viewport
real_world_example: A SaaS dashboard looks flawless on the developers' Chrome-on-Windows machines, but analytics show 22% of users are on Safari (macOS and iOS). After release, support reports the date-range filter doesn't open on Safari, a defect caused by a CSS/JS feature Safari handles differently. Had compatibility testing covered the top combinations from analytics (Chrome/Win, Safari/macOS, Safari/iOS, Chrome/Android), this environment-specific bug would have been caught before release instead of affecting a fifth of users.
step_by_step:
1. Pull real usage data (analytics): which browsers, versions, OSes, devices and resolutions do your users actually have?
2. Define the support policy: oldest and newest versions you officially support (backward/forward).
3. Build a prioritized compatibility matrix covering the combinations that represent the bulk of traffic plus risky edges.
4. Identify the critical flows to test on each combination (don't run everything everywhere).
5. Choose execution: real devices/device farm for top-priority combos, emulators/responsive mode for breadth.
6. Execute the critical flows on each target, watching layout, functionality and performance.
7. Log environment-specific defects with the exact browser/OS/device/version where they occur.
8. Re-test fixes on the same environment and keep the matrix updated as usage shifts.
common_mistakes:
Testing only on the developer's own browser/OS and assuming it works everywhere.
Trying to cover every possible combination instead of prioritizing by real usage and risk.
Relying solely on emulators for things that need real devices (touch, GPU, real network).
Forgetting backward compatibility (the oldest version you still officially support).
Logging a bug without specifying the exact environment, so developers can't reproduce it.
practical_use_case: Run before releases, after UI/CSS changes, and when adding support for new platforms. Any product with a diverse audience (web apps, mobile apps, sites) needs ongoing compatibility checks; QA maintains and runs the compatibility matrix each release cycle.
summary: Compatibility testing verifies the app works across the browsers, OSes, devices, resolutions and networks users actually have. Because fragmentation makes the full matrix enormous, you prioritize combinations by real usage data and risk, and cover both backward and forward compatibility. Test critical flows on real top-priority devices and use emulators for breadth.
example:
Prioritized compatibility matrix (derived from analytics, ~92% of traffic):

| # | Browser | Version | OS | Device | Resolution | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chrome | latest | Windows 11 | Desktop | 1920x1080 | P1 |
| 2 | Safari | latest | macOS 14 | Desktop | 1440x900 | P1 |
| 3 | Safari | latest | iOS 17 | iPhone 14 | 390x844 | P1 |
| 4 | Chrome | latest | Android 14 | Pixel 7 | 412x915 | P1 |
| 5 | Edge | latest | Windows 10 | Desktop | 1366x768 | P2 |
| 6 | Firefox | latest | Windows 11 | Desktop | 1920x1080 | P2 |
| 7 | Safari | iOS 15 (oldest supported) | iPhone SE | 375x667 | backward-compat | P2 |
| 8 | Chrome | beta (forward-compat) | Windows 11 | Desktop | 1920x1080 | P3 |

Critical flows per combo: login, dashboard load, run a report, export. Tool plan: real devices/device farm for P1, responsive DevTools + emulators for P2/P3.
interactive: Your analytics show: Chrome 60%, Safari (incl. iOS) 25%, Edge 8%, Firefox 5%, others 2%, with a 70/30 mobile/desktop split. Build a prioritized compatibility test plan: which 5 to 6 combinations would you test first and why?
expected_answer: A strong answer prioritizes by usage and the mobile-heavy split, covering ~90%+ of traffic: (1) Chrome / Android (mobile) and (2) Chrome / Windows (desktop) for the 60% Chrome base across both form factors; (3) Safari / iOS (mobile) and (4) Safari / macOS (desktop) for the 25% Safari base, with iOS weighted higher due to the 70% mobile split; (5) Edge / Windows for 8%; optionally (6) Firefox / Windows for 5%. It should justify the choices with the usage numbers and the mobile/desktop ratio, mention testing critical flows (not everything) on each, include at least one older-version (backward-compat) check, and note using a device farm for the top mobile combos. The key insight is risk/usage-based prioritization rather than attempting the full matrix.
homework: Choose a public website or app. Using its likely audience (or general 2025 market share if you can't get analytics), build a compatibility matrix of at least 8 prioritized browser/OS/device/resolution combinations with P1 to P3 priorities. Then actually test one critical flow (e.g. signup or checkout) on at least three different real or emulated environments and report any layout or functional differences you find.
expected_result: A prioritized compatibility matrix of 8+ combinations justified by usage/market share and a support policy (including at least one backward-compatibility entry), plus actual test results from running one critical flow on 3+ environments. Findings must specify the exact environment where each issue appears, demonstrating risk-based prioritization and real (or emulated) cross-environment execution rather than testing on a single browser.

quiz.question: Compatibility testing verifies that the app:
