answers: Works correctly across different browsers, OSes, devices and configurations

quiz.question: Because the full matrix of combinations is enormous, you should:
answers: Prioritize combinations by real usage data and risk
Test only on the developer's machine

quiz.question: Backward compatibility testing checks that the app:
answers: Still works on the oldest versions you officially support

quiz.question: When real touch behavior and GPU rendering matter, you should prefer:
answers: Real devices over emulators for the top-priority combinations

title: Cross-browser testing
short_description: Ensuring a web application looks and behaves consistently across the major browsers and their rendering engines.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Explain why rendering-engine and JavaScript differences cause cross-browser bugs.
Select a target browser set from market share and your audience.
Systematically check layout, functionality and console errors per browser.
Use cloud browser tools and DevTools to reproduce and report browser-specific defects.
theory:
Cross-browser testing is a focused part of compatibility testing that verifies a web application renders and functions correctly across different browsers, Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge, and their versions. Browsers differ because they use different rendering engines (Chrome and Edge use Blink, Safari uses WebKit, Firefox uses Gecko) and ship JavaScript and CSS features at different times. The same HTML/CSS/JS can therefore look or behave differently per browser.

Typical cross-browser bugs: CSS that uses a property one engine supports and another doesn't (e.g. certain fl/grid or backdrop-filter quirks), date/number formatting differences, a JavaScript API available in Chrome but not yet in Safari, fonts rendering at different weights, and form controls (date pickers, file inputs) that look and behave natively differently. Safari/WebKit, especially on iOS, is the most common source of surprises because it lags on some features and enforces stricter behaviors.

Scope your target browsers by market share and your audience analytics. A common baseline is the latest versions of Chrome, Safari, Firefox and Edge, plus the most-used mobile browsers (Safari on iOS, Chrome on Android), plus the oldest version your policy supports. You don't test obscure browsers your users don't have, you cover the set that represents the vast majority of your traffic.

For each target browser you systematically check three things: layout/visual (does it render correctly, no overlap, no clipping, correct fonts and spacing), functionality (do interactive elements, forms, AJAX calls and flows work), and the browser console (are there JavaScript errors or failed network requests visible in DevTools?). Comparing the same page side by side across browsers quickly reveals discrepancies.

Tooling makes this efficient. Cloud platforms (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs, LambdaTest) give instant access to real browser/OS combinations without maintaining a device lab, and automation frameworks (Selenium Grid, Playwright, Cypress) can run the same test script across multiple browsers. Modern CSS practices, feature detection, and tools like 'caniuse.com' help prevent issues, but testing is still required to catch the real rendering differences.
key_terms: rendering engine (Blink/WebKit/Gecko), browser market share, console errors, feature support, polyfill, responsive layout, Selenium Grid / Playwright, caniuse
real_world_example: A marketing landing page uses a CSS 'backdrop-filter' blur for its hero and an HTML5 date input on the signup form. On Chrome and Edge (Blink) it looks great. In cross-browser testing you open it on Safari (WebKit): the blur renders differently and the native date input shows a completely different control, while on an older Firefox the blur isn't applied at all, leaving low-contrast white text on a busy image. The DevTools console on Safari also shows a JS error from an unsupported API. You log three browser-specific defects, each tagged with the exact browser and version, that would otherwise have hurt conversions for Safari and Firefox users.
step_by_step:
1. From analytics/market share, define your target browser set (latest Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge + key mobile + oldest supported).
2. List the critical pages and flows to verify on each browser.
3. Establish the reference look/behavior (usually the primary browser, e.g. Chrome).
4. For each target browser, check layout/visual rendering against the reference.
5. Check functionality: forms, buttons, AJAX, navigation, media.
6. Open DevTools console/network on each browser and note JS errors or failed requests.
7. Use a cloud platform (BrowserStack/LambdaTest) for browsers/OSes you can't run locally, especially Safari/iOS.
8. Log each discrepancy with the exact browser, version and OS, plus a screenshot and console output; retest fixes on that same browser.
common_mistakes:
Only testing in Chrome and assuming Safari/Firefox behave identically.
Ignoring the browser console, missing JavaScript errors that break functionality on one engine.
Not checking mobile Safari (iOS WebKit), which is where many real users and many bugs are.
Reporting 'it's broken' without the exact browser and version, so developers can't reproduce it.
Testing obscure browsers nobody uses while skipping the high-share ones.
practical_use_case: Run on every front-end release and after any CSS/JS change for any public web application. Cross-browser checks are a routine part of web QA, often partly automated across browsers in CI and partly done manually for visual verification.
summary: Cross-browser testing verifies a web app renders and works consistently across browsers that use different engines (Blink, WebKit, Gecko) and ship features at different times. For each target browser, chosen by market share, you check layout, functionality and the DevTools console. Safari/WebKit, especially on iOS, is the most common source of surprises.
example:
Cross-browser test matrix and a filed defect:

Reference browser: Chrome 124 / Windows 11.
Targets: Safari 17 / macOS, Safari 17 / iOS 17, Firefox 125 / Windows, Edge 124 / Windows, Chrome / Android 14.
Page under test: /signup
Checks per browser: (a) layout renders correctly, (b) date-of-birth picker works, (c) form submits, (d) console clean.

Results summary:
- Chrome/Win: all pass (reference).
- Edge/Win: all pass.
- Firefox/Win: layout OK, but hero blur not applied (low contrast). FAIL (a).
- Safari/macOS: date input shows different native control; submit works; console error 'X is not a function'. FAIL (d).
- Safari/iOS: 'Sign up' button overlaps the footer at 390px width. FAIL (a).

Defect CB-07 (Severity High):
Title: Signup button overlaps footer on Safari iOS 17 (iPhone 14, 390x844)
Environment: Safari 17, iOS 17, iPhone 14, portrait, 390px viewport
Steps: Open /signup on Safari iOS -> scroll to the bottom.
Expected: 'Sign up' button sits above the footer with spacing.
Actual: button overlaps the footer links, making them untappable.
Evidence: screenshot attached; not reproducible on Chrome/Android.
interactive: You must cross-browser test a checkout page. List the target browsers you'd choose and, for each, the three categories of checks you'd perform. Then give one realistic browser-specific bug you might find and how you'd report it.
expected_answer: A strong answer picks a sensible target set (latest Chrome, Safari macOS, Safari iOS, Firefox, Edge, Chrome Android, plus the oldest supported version) justified by market share/audience, and states the three check categories for each: (1) layout/visual rendering vs the reference browser, (2) functionality (form fields, payment widget, buttons, AJAX), and (3) console/network errors in DevTools. A realistic bug: 'On Safari iOS the Apple/credit-card payment iframe doesn't load due to a WebKit cookie/storage restriction, so checkout can't complete', reported with the exact browser+version+OS+device, reproduction steps, expected vs actual, a screenshot and the console error, and a note that it does not reproduce on Chrome. The answer should emphasize engine differences (WebKit vs Blink) as the root cause.
homework: Take any public web page with a form (signup, contact, checkout). Test it on at least three different browsers (use BrowserStack/LambdaTest free trial or local browsers, including at least one Safari/WebKit). For each browser, check layout, functionality and the console, and produce a cross-browser report listing every discrepancy with the exact browser/version and a screenshot.
expected_result: A cross-browser report covering at least three browsers including a WebKit (Safari) target, with per-browser results across the three check categories (layout, functionality, console). Every discrepancy must be tagged with the exact browser, version and OS, include a screenshot, and ideally note the likely rendering-engine cause. The submission should reflect market-share-based browser selection rather than arbitrary or obscure browsers.

quiz.question: The root cause of most cross-browser bugs is that browsers:
answers: Use the same engine but different colors
Use different rendering engines and ship CSS/JS features at different times

quiz.question: Which rendering engine does Safari use?
Besides layout and functionality, what should you always check per browser?
answers: The browser console for JavaScript and network errors

quiz.question: When choosing which browsers to test, you should primarily base the set on:
answers: Market share and your audience's analytics
Browsers nobody uses, to be safe

title: Mobile testing basics
short_description: The fundamentals of testing native, hybrid and mobile-web apps across devices, OS versions, gestures, networks and interruptions.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Distinguish native, hybrid and mobile-web apps and how each is tested.
List mobile-specific test conditions (gestures, orientation, interruptions, permissions, battery, network).
Build a device/OS coverage matrix using fragmentation and usage data.
Test install/update/uninstall flows and app-store-relevant behaviors.
theory:
Mobile testing covers applications running on smartphones and tablets, and it adds many concerns that desktop web testing does not have. First, know the app type: native apps are built for a specific platform (Swift/Kotlin) and installed from the App Store/Play Store; mobile web apps run in the phone browser; hybrid apps wrap web content in a native shell (e.g. React Native, Flutter, Cordova). The type affects how you test (e.g. native apps need install/update flows; mobile web needs browser compatibility).

Mobile fragmentation is severe, especially on Android: thousands of device models, many screen sizes and densities, and multiple OS versions in active use. You can't test them all, so you build a device matrix prioritized by your users' real device/OS share, covering top devices, a range of screen sizes, the latest and oldest supported OS versions, and at least one low-end device for performance.

Mobile-specific conditions must be explicitly tested: touch gestures (tap, long-press, swipe, pinch-to-zoom), orientation changes (portrait/landscape and rotation mid-task), and interruptions, an incoming call, SMS, alarm, notification, or the app being backgrounded and resumed, must not lose data or crash. Permissions (camera, location, contacts, notifications) must be requested and handled correctly when granted AND denied.

Network and resource conditions matter more on mobile. Apps must behave on slow or flaky connections (3G, edge), on Wi-Fi to cellular handoff, and offline (graceful messaging, queued actions). You also watch battery drain, memory usage and app size, and verify install, update (including migrating old data) and uninstall flows, plus behavior after the OS kills a backgrounded app.

Execution uses real devices and cloud device farms (BrowserStack App Live, Sauce Labs, Firebase Test Lab) plus emulators/simulators. Emulators are great for quick functional checks and breadth; real devices are essential for gestures, camera, GPS, performance, push notifications and true network behavior. A practical strategy: real devices for top-priority models and critical flows, emulators for wider coverage.
key_terms: native / hybrid / mobile-web, device fragmentation, gestures, orientation, interruptions, permissions, device farm (emulator vs real device), install/update/uninstall flow
real_world_example: You're testing a ride-hailing native app. Beyond the booking flow, mobile-specific tests catch the real bugs: when a call comes in mid-booking the app loses the selected destination; rotating to landscape on the map screen crashes the app; denying location permission shows a blank screen instead of a 'please enable location' prompt; and on a slow 3G connection the 'Find driver' spinner hangs forever with no timeout. None of these would show up in plain functional testing on a fast Wi-Fi emulator, they require deliberately testing interruptions, orientation, permissions and network conditions on real devices.
step_by_step:
1. Identify the app type (native, hybrid, mobile web) and the supported platforms/OS versions.
2. Pull device/OS usage data and build a prioritized device matrix (top models, screen sizes, oldest+newest OS, one low-end device).
3. Test core functional flows first on a primary device.
4. Test gestures and orientation: tap/swipe/pinch, rotate mid-task, both orientations.
5. Test interruptions: incoming call/SMS/notification, backgrounding and resuming, OS killing the app.
6. Test permissions in both granted and denied states.
7. Test network conditions: Wi-Fi, slow 3G, offline, and handoff between them.
8. Test install/update/uninstall, including data migration on update; watch performance, battery and app size.
9. Run critical flows on real top-priority devices and use a device farm/emulators for breadth; log defects with exact device + OS version.
common_mistakes:
Testing only on an emulator or one high-end phone and ignoring fragmentation and low-end devices.
Forgetting mobile-specific scenarios: interruptions, orientation changes, and denied permissions.
Not testing on poor or offline networks, so failures appear only in the real world.
Ignoring install/update flows, especially data migration when users upgrade the app.
Logging a mobile bug without the exact device model and OS version, making it hard to reproduce.
practical_use_case: Any team shipping a mobile app runs these checks every release: functional flows plus the mobile-specific layer (gestures, interruptions, permissions, networks, devices). QA maintains the device matrix and runs critical flows on real devices via a device farm each cycle.
summary: Mobile testing adds concerns desktop web does not have: device/OS fragmentation, gestures, orientation, interruptions, permissions, variable networks, battery and install/update flows. You build a usage-based device matrix and test critical flows on real top-priority devices, using emulators for breadth. Native, hybrid and mobile-web apps each need slightly different coverage.
example:
Mobile-specific test checklist (native app, run per release):

Device matrix (prioritized by usage):
- iPhone 14 / iOS 17 (P1), iPhone SE / iOS 15 oldest supported (P2)
- Pixel 7 / Android 14 (P1), Samsung A14 low-end / Android 13 (P2)

Mobile-specific checks:
[ ] Tap, long-press, swipe and pinch-to-zoom work where expected.
[ ] Rotate device mid-task: layout adapts, no data loss, no crash (portrait + landscape).
[ ] Incoming call during a key flow: app pauses and resumes with state intact.
[ ] Background the app for 5 min, then resume: session and unsaved data preserved.
[ ] Deny location permission: clear prompt shown, app does not crash; grant it: feature works.
[ ] Slow 3G: actions show loading state and time out gracefully; Offline: clear offline message.
[ ] Wi-Fi -> cellular handoff mid-request: request completes or retries cleanly.
[ ] Fresh install, then update from previous version: existing user data migrates correctly.
[ ] App size, battery drain and memory within expected limits on the low-end device.
Log every failure with device model + OS version + steps.
interactive: For a mobile banking app's 'transfer money' flow, list six mobile-specific test scenarios (beyond the basic functional steps) that you would run, and the expected result for each.
expected_answer: A strong answer focuses on mobile-specific conditions, not just the happy path: (1) Rotate the device while entering the amount -> layout adapts and the entered amount is preserved, no crash. (2) Incoming call mid-transfer -> app pauses; on return the form state is intact and the transfer is not duplicated. (3) Background the app at the confirmation screen, reopen after 5 min -> session handled securely (e.g. re-auth) with no data loss or double-submit. (4) Deny biometric/notification permission -> graceful fallback (PIN) rather than a crash; grant it -> works. (5) Slow 3G or offline at submit -> clear loading/timeout/offline message, the transfer is not silently lost or duplicated. (6) Tap 'Confirm' twice quickly on a laggy connection -> only one transfer is created (idempotency). Each scenario should pair the action with a concrete expected result and ideally specify a device/OS.
homework: Choose a mobile app on your own phone. Build a small device matrix (even if you only have one device, state what you'd ideally cover and why) and run a mobile-specific test session covering gestures, orientation, at least one interruption, a denied permission, and a poor/offline network condition. Produce a report of at least four findings, each with the exact device, OS version and reproduction steps.
expected_result: A report from a real mobile-specific test session covering gestures, orientation change, an interruption, a denied-permission case, and a degraded/offline network, plus a (at least conceptual) prioritized device matrix justified by fragmentation/usage. At least four findings should be documented with exact device model, OS version, reproduction steps and expected vs actual, showing the student tested beyond the basic functional happy path.

quiz.question: Which app type is installed from the App Store/Play Store and built for a specific platform?
An 'interruption' test on mobile would include:
answers: An incoming call, SMS or notification during a key flow

quiz.question: Why must you test on a range of real devices, not just one emulator?
answers: Fragmentation, gestures, camera/GPS, performance and real networks behave differently across real devices

quiz.question: When a user denies the location permission, a well-tested app should:
answers: Show a clear prompt/fallback and keep working without crashing

title: Web testing basics
short_description: The core checks for any web application: functionality, UI, links, forms, sessions, security basics, performance and the browser network/console.
learning_goals:
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Run a structured web test across functionality, UI, links, forms, sessions and errors.
Use browser DevTools (Network and Console) to inspect requests, status codes and JS errors.
Interpret common HTTP status codes (200, 301/302, 400, 401, 403, 404, 500) during testing.
Check basic web security and performance concerns that QA owns.
theory:
Web testing is the testing of applications that run in a browser, and it ties together many types you've learned into a practical routine. The core areas are: functionality (features work per spec), UI/visual (layout, fonts, responsiveness), navigation and links (no broken links, correct routing), forms and validation, cookies/sessions/authentication, basic security, performance, and cross-browser/compatibility. A solid web tester moves through these areas methodically for each page and flow.

Forms are a major focus. For every form you test field validation (required fields, formats, lengths, boundaries), positive and negative submissions, error messages, default values, and what happens on double-submit or back-button after submit. Links and navigation come next: check that internal links go to the right place, external links open correctly, there are no broken links (which return HTTP 404), and that deep links and the browser back/forward buttons behave.

The browser's DevTools are the web tester's most important instrument. The Network tab shows every request, its HTTP method, status code, payload and timing, so you can confirm an API returned 200 (OK), spot a 404 (not found), a 401 (unauthorized) or 403 (forbidden) on protected resources, a 400 (bad request) on invalid input, or a 500 (server error) that indicates a backend bug. A 301/302 indicates a redirect. The Console tab surfaces JavaScript errors and warnings that may break behavior even when the page looks fine. Always test with DevTools open.

Sessions, cookies and authentication are core web concerns. Verify that login sets a session/cookie, that protected pages redirect to login when you're logged out (often a 302 to /login or a 401), that logout truly ends the session, that sessions time out, and that you can't access another user's data by changing an ID in the URL (a basic broken-access-control / IDOR check). These overlap with the security basics QA is expected to catch: also watch for inputs reflected without escaping (potential XSS) and obvious injection points.

Performance and responsiveness round it out. Check page load and key interactions feel fast (use the Network waterfall and tools like Lighthouse for LCP/CLS), that the layout is responsive across viewport sizes, and that images and assets aren't broken or oversized. Web testing is iterative: you run these checks per release, across the priority browsers, with DevTools open, and log defects with the exact URL, status code, console output and steps.
key_terms: DevTools (Network/Console), HTTP status codes (200/301/302/400/401/403/404/500), form validation, cookies/session/authentication, broken links, responsive layout, IDOR / broken access control, Lighthouse / Core Web Vitals
real_world_example: You're testing a web dashboard's 'export report' feature. The page looks fine, but with DevTools open you notice the export request returns HTTP 500, and the Console shows a TypeError. Digging further, you log out and directly visit /reports/123; instead of redirecting to login it loads the report (a 200 where you expected a 302 to /login), revealing a broken-access-control bug. You also find an external 'Help' link that 404s and a contact form that accepts a 10,000-character message with no limit. One short, DevTools-driven web test session surfaced a server error, a security flaw, a broken link and a missing validation, defects spanning functionality, security and forms.
step_by_step:
1. Open the app in a priority browser with DevTools (Network + Console) open.
2. Smoke the critical flows first (load, login, key actions) and watch status codes.
3. Test each form: required fields, formats, boundaries, positive and negative submits, error messages, double-submit and back-button.
4. Check navigation and links: internal routing, external links, no 404s, back/forward behavior, deep links.
5. Test sessions/auth: login sets a session, protected pages redirect/deny when logged out, logout ends the session, session timeout, can't access another user's record by changing the URL id.
6. Watch the Network tab for unexpected status codes (404, 401/403, 400, 500) and the Console for JS errors throughout.
7. Check responsiveness across viewport sizes and run Lighthouse for performance/accessibility basics.
8. Verify across priority browsers (cross-browser) and log each defect with URL, steps, status code and console output.
common_mistakes:
Testing with DevTools closed and missing failed requests, wrong status codes and JS console errors.
Only testing happy-path form submissions and skipping validation, boundaries and negative cases.
Not checking access control (e.g. changing an id in the URL to view another user's data).
Overlooking broken links (404s) and external links that don't open correctly.
Logging a bug without the URL, the HTTP status code, or the console error, so it can't be reproduced.
practical_use_case: This is the daily routine for QA on any web product: every feature, fix and release is verified with this structured pass, DevTools open, covering functionality, forms, links, sessions, basic security and performance, across priority browsers.
summary: Web testing is a structured pass over functionality, UI, links, forms, sessions/auth, basic security and performance for browser-based apps. The DevTools Network and Console tabs are the key instruments: read HTTP status codes (200, 301/302, 400, 401, 403, 404, 500) and watch for JS errors. Always log defects with the exact URL, status code, console output and steps.
example:
Web testing bug report driven by DevTools:

ID: WEB-058
Title: Logged-out user can open another user's report (broken access control)
Severity: Critical
Environment: Chrome 124 / Windows 11, staging build 6.1.0
Preconditions: Two accounts exist; report id 123 belongs to user A.
Steps:
1. Log in as user B, confirm you only see your own reports.
2. Log out completely.
3. In the address bar, go directly to /reports/123 (user A's report).
Expected result:
- Request is rejected: redirect to /login (HTTP 302) or HTTP 401/403.
- The report is NOT shown.
Actual result:
- Network tab shows GET /reports/123 -> HTTP 200.
- User A's full report is rendered to a logged-out visitor.
Evidence: Network tab screenshot showing the 200 response; URL bar screenshot.
Notes: Also reproduces by changing /reports/123 to /reports/124 while logged in as B (IDOR).
interactive: Open any website with DevTools and describe what you would check in the Network and Console tabs during a login flow. List four HTTP status codes you might see and what each would tell you as a tester.
expected_answer: A strong answer explains using the Network tab to watch the login POST and subsequent requests, and the Console for JS errors. For the login flow: a successful login's API call should return 200 (or set a session cookie and 302-redirect to the dashboard); submitting wrong credentials should return 401 (unauthorized) with a friendly error; visiting a protected page while logged out should 302-redirect to /login or return 401/403. Four status codes with meaning, e.g.: 200 OK (request succeeded), 302 Found (redirect, e.g. to /login or dashboard), 401 Unauthorized (not authenticated, e.g. bad credentials/expired session), 404 Not Found (resource/endpoint missing, e.g. a broken link), and optionally 500 (server error indicating a backend bug). The answer should connect each code to a tester decision (e.g. a 500 on submit = log a backend defect; a 200 on a protected resource while logged out = a security defect).
homework: Pick a public web app with a login and a form. With DevTools open, run a structured web test covering functionality, at least one form's validation (positive + negative + boundary), navigation/links, sessions/auth (including trying to access a protected page while logged out), and the Network/Console. Produce a report with at least five findings, each including the URL, the HTTP status code observed, any console error, and reproduction steps.
expected_result: A structured web test report covering functionality, form validation (with negative/boundary cases), links/navigation, and session/auth behavior, produced with DevTools open. At least five findings should each include the exact URL, the observed HTTP status code, any console/network error, and reproduction steps, demonstrating the student can use DevTools and interpret status codes (e.g. spotting a 404 broken link, a 500 server error, or an access-control issue) rather than only eyeballing the page.

quiz.question: In the browser DevTools, the Network tab is most useful for:
answers: Seeing each request's HTTP method, status code, payload and timing

quiz.question: An API request returns HTTP 500 when you submit a form. This indicates:
answers: A redirect to the login page
A server-side error (a backend defect to log)

quiz.question: While logged out, opening a protected page should ideally:
answers: Redirect to login (302) or return 401/403 and NOT show the data

quiz.question: Changing an id in the URL (e.g. /reports/123 to /reports/124) and seeing another user's data is an example of:
answers: Broken access control / IDOR, a security defect
